event: Predella panel depicting courtly judgment or audience scenes
symbols: enthroned figure — authority and judgment, whether secular or divine
red canopy at left — royal or judicial authority
messenger or petitioner figures — those who seek justice or bring tidings
dog — fidelity, or in some contexts, the Gentiles
description: A narrow horizontal predella panel set within an ornate gilded frame with egg-and-dart molding. The panel depicts a continuous narrative across multiple episodes. At the far left, a group of figures stands before a red-draped throne or canopy in an architectural interior. In the center, a figure in dark clothing raises something overhead, possibly delivering a decree or sentence. At right, an enthroned elderly figure in rich green and pink robes sits on steps, receiving or judging, while other figures in varied medieval garments approach. A small white dog sits near the right-center.
theological_description: Predella panels typically narrate episodes from the lives of saints or from Scripture, serving as a visual narrative complement to the larger altarpiece above. Without certain identification of the specific narrative, the courtly judgment theme resonates with numerous biblical scenes of judgment and supplication before rulers -- from Esther before Ahasuerus to Christ before Pilate. The presence of an angel or putto above the central action may indicate divine intervention in human affairs, a common theme in hagiographic narratives. The multi-episode format reflects the medieval understanding of time as simultaneously unfolding under God's providential gaze.
